Comment: Bus passes are tricky to get hold of - you won't be able to get them from the kiosks dotted around the city as they say in the guide books. The only place you can get them is at 6, Kneza Mihaila, next to the Belgrade Cultural Centre. Go through the ordinary glass front door, then up the stairs to the second floor. We went there a few weeks ago and they didn't have any 3-day passes, only 1-day (which you can't buy in advance, they have to be used on that day), 7-day or 30-day. They 7-day pass costs 800 dinars.

Comment: THINGS YOU CAN DO FOR FREE IN BELGRADE >>> As of 1/1/2025, public transportation is free (except the A1 shuttle bus from the airport and "E" mini buses that you may see here and there). Buses, trams, trolley-buses, and electric buses are free, like a hop on - hop off system. Bus 72 from the airport to Zeleni Venac (wider city center) is also free - with just a short walk to the main pedestrian street which is in the center. Entrance to the Belgrade Fortress and the Saint Sava Temple is free. Entrance to all religious objects is free. Entrance to all parks is free (except the Botanical Garden). Some museums offer free admission on certain days. For example, the National Museum is free on Sundays, others may be giving free admission on other days, you have to Google it, depending on your interests (Belgrade has 52 registered museums, small and large). Entrance to the Ada Lake/beach is free. So, quite a bit of free stuff to do. You can always sit on a park bench and do people-watching, enjoy the sunset at the fortress or at the very impressive Monument to the Unknown Hero at Avala Mountain, walk and ride as much as you want... Tap water is safe and potable, so you don't have to keep on buying bottled water. You can refill at your apartment or hotel, or at that public fountain in the main pedestrian street. If you eat at bakeries and fast food places, you can get a meal for a few bucks. So, done right, Belgrade can be quite affordable.

Comment: Bródno (9th/10th century): A small fortified settlement (gród) in the north-eastern part of modern Warsaw, destroyed around 1040. Kamion (11th century): A settlement near the present-day Warszawa Wschodnia station. Jazdów (12th/13th century): A nearby fortified, residential, and administrative settlement that was destroyed, leading to the development of the new site. Warsaw has existed since approximately 1300, serving primarily as the capital of Poland (or its predecessor states) for most of its history, though it was occupied by Prussia, Russia, and Germany during the partitions (1795–1918) and WWII. It is currently the capital of the Republic of Poland, a role it officially held from 1596–1795 and 1918–present. Key Historical Affiliations (Approx. 1300–Present): c. 1300–1526: Duchy of Masovia (independent/Polish vassal): Founded as a trading post, later becoming the capital of the Duchy of Masovia. 1526–1795: Kingdom of Poland/Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth: Incorporated into Poland (1526), becoming the seat of the Sejm (1569) and the official capital (1596). 1795–1806: Kingdom of Prussia: Annexed by Prussia during the Third Partition of Poland, becoming part of the province of South Prussia. 1807–1815: Duchy of Warsaw: Created by Napoleon Bonaparte as a French puppet state. 1815–1918: Russian Empire (Congress Poland): Following Napoleon’s defeat, it became the capital of a kingdom linked to Russia, eventually losing autonomy. 1918–1939: Republic of Poland (Second Republic): Restored as the capital of independent Poland. It was a part of Germany in 1939-1945 Then a part of Poland for the rest of its life

Comment: To be honest, I find Warsaw quite boring for a capital city. The service culture can feel very rigid, even compared to other European cities, and some people get upset when you speak English. Many museums are also difficult to fully enjoy if you don’t speak Polish. Poles are also very unfriendly even when compared to other Europeans. Of course not everyone is the same, I have one Polish friend and she is great. That said, it’s very safe. The only crimes I’ve really heard about involve locals having their cars stolen. But I’ve also encountered racism and homophobia that reminded me more of rednecks from the USA than of a capital in the EU. The city lacks charm and the people tend to be not friendly and borderline rude at times but very proud.

Comment: From my personal experience, the further north you go in Europe, the harder it gets to build genuine human connections. Poland is already leaning that way, close to Scandinavia, even the weather feels almost the same. Meanwhile, in South-Eastern Europe and the Balkans, friendships form much more naturally. Of course, you won’t always get the same level of order or infrastructure you see in Poland or Scandinavia, but you will find warmer vibes, better energy, and sunnier skies. I honestly believe weather shapes people more than we realize. In Central and Northern Europe, you’ve got long, dark winters and endless rain, people stay inside, become more withdrawn and over time even a bit alienated or depressed. They also become "work alcoholics" as they mistakenly believe that money will fix the original problem. If you feel depressed no matter where you are, that’s an inner issue you need to face. But if it only happens in certain countries, then maybe it’s not you, maybe it’s the vibe of that place. And i will take it even further and say it can actually be a sign you shouldn't be there to begin with, that you are not meant to be there. Not every land resonates with everyone’s mentality or energy.

Comment: You're an Asian guy from LA who has been in Warsaw 90 days from what I understand. I don't know what you were expecting, but it is going to take time to adjust. In the meantime, you will experience loneliness and possibly even alienation. I'm of Polish descent and spent some time voting Poland about four years ago. Although I have relatives near Krakow, I still experienced culture shock, in part because of the language barrier. My relatives were nice but they live in a remote area about 30 minutes from Old Town Krakow. I enjoyed spending weekends in Old Town. I think I could spend several months there and enjoy it. I think Warsaw would be a tougher nut to crack because of its size. If you met a nice Polish girl, that would probably make things easier if you intend to remain in Poland. I found Polish people to be polite, but making new friends anywhere takes time and effort. I wish you luck.

Comment: I liked that you spoke so openly about what I like and dislike about Warsaw. You touched on many things with which I may or may not agree. It's also true that it's quite different when you visit and when you live in the capital. I'm one of those people who goes to Warsaw two or three times a month. For me, Warsaw is a completely different (and better) world than Kielce. It's expensive there, especially renting an apartment, but I have to admit that compared to Kielce, at least that's my impression, it's easier to make contact with someone on the street. There are many career opportunities there, but it all depends on your skills and how many languages ​​you know. I'd like to move to Warsaw, but I don't know if I'd be able to function there because I have health problems, especially with my bad knees. Maybe I'll move there someday:-) Best regards and have a nice Sunday:-)!
